title: 211 Advocacy Day: Visiting the Oregon State Capitol - 211info
text: Nickie Carter, Piseth Pich, Cara Kangas, Dan Herman at the Oregon State Capitol, February 2025 On National 211 Day (February 11), our team met at the Oregon State Capitol with a mission to educate and advocate. We wanted to introduce new legislators to 211info and reintroduce ourselves to those already familiar with our work. Many serve on committees that directly impact our funding, making it crucial to highlight why 211info is an essential service—and why we’re requesting $3.084 million for the next biennium. We also wanted representatives to fully understand not just what the 211 service is but how our system helps the public and provider agencies navigate resources both daily and during emergency incidents. And most importantly, understand the impact of 211 as a statewide system at the local level. Our team met with 15 representatives and their staff from across Oregon to discuss how the requested funding will: Maintain 24/7 operations: Over 37% of our general calls come in outside traditional business hours, and incident response calls are even more frequent during evenings and weekends. Strengthen emergency preparedness and incident response: to ensure we remain a nimble and reliable partner during disasters. Expand our Community Engagement Coordinator (CEC) outreach program: adding local support in Lane/Douglas, Marion/Polk/Yamhill, and Klamath/Lake regions. To unpack the experience, we sat down with Cara Kangas, Director of Partnerships, to discuss key takeaways, common misconceptions, and why this funding is so critical. Q: What stood out during your conversations? Did anything surprise you? A few things stood out. First, only two legislators we met with had never heard of 211, which was a win! But a couple didn’t realize we serve the entire state and assumed we were only in the Portland metro. It’s true we started there, but we’ve been statewide since 2013, serving all 36 counties in Oregon. Our local outreach teams ensure we have accurate resource information for rural and urban communities. While most knew about our general information and referral services, not everyone knew how crucial 211info is to emergency incident response efforts across the state. One of my favorite moments was when our board member, Piseth Pitch , who also works for a fire department, explained how his department relies on 211 to ease the burden of non-emergency calls—because people often overutilize 911 for non-emergency needs. Seeing legislators connect the dots on one of the many ways we support emergency response was powerful. The biggest surprise was that a few thought we were a government agency. We’re an independent nonprofit, and while we receive some public funding through local government contracts for specific programs, we rely on diverse funding sources to keep our services running. Q: What were the most common questions or misconceptions? There were four key areas where questions continued to come up throughout the day: 1: “Why does 211 need to be open 24/7?” Because crises don’t only happen during business hours. Think about disasters—floods, wildfires, severe storms, power outages, etc. Plus, 24/7 operations create efficiencies and lower overnight costs for our partners. It’s also essential for access since over 37% of our general calls come in outside traditional business hours. 2: “How do you decide where to expand outreach efforts?” We look at gaps in service, call trends, and community needs. Right now, Lane/Douglas, Marion/Polk/Yamhill, and Klamath/Lake regions (individual CECs cover more than one county) would benefit most from additional on-the-ground support. 3: “Does 211 work for people in rural areas?” We work alongside local organizations to strengthen networks of care and amplify messaging. And this is exactly why we have local team members across the state who partner with local people and organizations . We work hard to fit in with and support the local provider landscape. We don’t just direct people to services—we strengthen community connections. That’s also why we’ve launched regional partnership roundtables to bring providers together to improve coordination and close resource gaps. 4: “If you already have contracts with some state agencies, why would we need to fund you directly?” Our existing contracts are only for specific programs with limited scope of work, like our Child Care Referrals and Foster Care Support. Our current request is more broad and will support our operations and ability to remain a streamlined, cost-effective way to find help. Direct funding allows us to stay nimble in emergencies and continue serving as a simple, centralized way for the public to connect to resources. Q: What was the most fun part of the day? Getting to see how different representatives decorate their offices! It was refreshing to meet face-to-face with the people shaping Oregon’s policies. That human connection is precisely what 211info is all about. The most fun was bringing our statewide leadership team together in one place. We’re spread out across the state, so having in-person time was rare and valuable. Q: What’s one thing you hope legislators will remember about 211info? That we are a person-centered service. Our compassionate team doesn’t just read off lists of resources—we help problem-solve, advocate, and help people navigate complex and often overwhelming situations. Just like legislators, we show up for our communities every day. Investing in 211info isn’t just funding a helpline—it’s strengthening the safety and support system for our entire state. Final Thoughts 211 Advocacy Day was a powerful reminder of why our work matters and how we fit into Oregon’s broader public service system. The best part? Seeing lightbulb moments when legislators grasped how we support other agencies and the public—especially during crises. We left the Capitol energized, knowing each conversation helped strengthen understanding. Call to Action: If you believe in our work, please contact your legislators to share your support and urge them to advocate for 211info. To find your State Senator and Representative, please use this link and enter your zip code. About 211info 211info is an independent nonprofit organization that operates the 211 system for Oregon and Southwest Washington. Funded by state and municipal contracts, healthcare systems, community-based organizations, and community donors, 211info helps people navigate essential services like food, housing, and child care. In addition to supporting basic needs, 211info plays a key role during emergency and incident response, including wildfires, severe weather, communicable diseases, industrial contamination, and more. During the COVID-19 pandemic, 211info served as the statewide hub for all health, vaccine, and public information inquiries . 211info connects people to the help they need.
